name: Akelitys, Andrew J. E.
description: "Andie Johnie Eddie Akie" came to us from Poly with one purpose in mind. He wants to be a medico. During the time that he has been with us, we have learned to like and respect him as an earnest, honest, and hard worker. Akie found time to play lacrosse, so that when we saw him with a cut up face, we knew that he was in the thick of a "Red Eagle" fight. With his fighting spirit and aptness for study, we feel sure that Akie will reach his "goal" — '25 wishes him success.

name: Alperin, Jacob
description: Jake came to us from that delightful, darky town, along the Mississippi, Memphis, to study medicine. Since, he has had many occasions to look the profession over at first hand — much to his discomfort. But he persists in pursuing medicine. He likes his Chem. and Biology, but his favorite pastime is translating Mexican orders for incubator catalogues. Jake is a quiet fellow with a sense of humor, and sufficient unto himself. Maybe you don't know where he is, but Paul Cohen always does!

name: Ashley, Raymond L.
description: Friends, allow us to introduce "Ray" Ashley, the other sheik of the present Engineering class. Ray's "Kollegiate Kut" attire and neatly combed hair have kept him well ahead of the rest of the field in the race for female admiration. Indeed, it is the firm belief of his associates that he is the logical successor of Valentino, when the latter becomes old and decrepit. Besides these sheiking propensities, "Ray" has also shown marked ability in scholastic endeavors and with such accomplishments, his success in the future is assured.

name: Ashton, Robert J.
description: A man such as "Tubby" would inspire any poet to write: "Of all the sad words of tongue or pen, the saddest are these, it might have been." Tubby might have been an excellent basketball manager, but he didn't have a basketball team to manage. He is one of those popular good-natured youths who doesn't lose flesh over anything. On an offhand guess we could say that he eats a lot also. Unlike most fat men, Tubby has no gravy spots down the front of his vest. We don't know of anything great he has ever done except being a good fellow, but he has succeeded admirably at that. We predict an opulent and corpulent life for Tubby.

name: Babcock, Paul M.
description: When the Class of '25 enumerates its social lions, tea-fighters, and he-flappers, it is safe to say that Paul M. will lead all the rest. According to the class statistics, this man has not missed a social event during the past four years. Paul's ambition in life is to be able to saunter down the Champs Elysée and to say "oui, oui," without an English accent. This should make him pre-eminent as a floor-walker in a department store.

name: Baker, Ralph B.
description: "Bake" is a quiet, unassuming, likeable fellow. He is one of the few men who have stuck to the engineering course for four years. There were sixty men that started as Freshmen in engineering at the same time as Baker. Only twelve of these will receive engineering degrees with us. The others for one reason or another have dropped out. By his quiet persistence, Bake has succeeded where so many others have failed. He intends to continue in the chemistry department for a Ph.D.

name: Baker, John T.
description: Johnny Baker—such a euphonious name inspires one to song, and indeed this well-liked manager of lacrosse can with little difficulty be associated with song. The perfect combination of "wine, women and song" is not one of John's favorites—he cannot accept such a doctrine in its entirety, but he really does favor parts of it. We know he likes song, and we've never even seen him speak to a woman. Quiet, cheerful, unassuming and good-natured, he has become well-known in spite of his apparent lack of interest in such things. When in the years to come, the Class of '25 is searched for men who have acquitted themselves well, John will not be found among the least of them.

name: Bald, George H.
clubs: Δ Γ Φ θ Τ Β Π
description: To send "Baldy" into the world as an electrical engineer is like casting pearls before swine. To think of him repairing doorbells is preposterous. His true station in life should be that of a dancing partner at the Casino. With the aid of his blue roadster he would have an easy life. During his stay at the University his nocturnal ramblings have had no bad effect upon his scholarship, hence we vote him a genius, namely, one who can keep girls and calculus upon his mind at the same time.

name: Bartholomaeus, John
clubs: R. O. T. C. Officer, Second Lieutenant
description: "Bart" is one engineer who knows of every dance at the University. He always has time to step out, even the night before a "big quiz." He firmly intends to follow electrical engineering and will probably enter upon work immediately. We advise Bart not to establish his own firm too soon, for it is but fair to give the other man a chance. When it comes to experience he has it; ask him about his trip to Pittsburgh last summer. In June, Westinghouse will get a good man, and he should do us credit.

name: Bates, F. Tyler
clubs: R. O. T. C. Officer, Second Lieutenant, 4
description: From the way Bates plays bridge one would think him an engineer. Watching women when he is in the vicinity makes one wonder why he is a bachelor. When he dances, one considers that he is wasting his talent at college. However, when he plays lacrosse one can forgive him almost anything—even Ten Hills. It is rumored throughout the country that Bates will have a write-up in "Donnybrook Fair" and this is not at all surprising when one is also informed of the companion rumor—that certain Residence Halls near 24th Street are to be draped in mourning the day Tyler gets his degree.

name: Brewer, George
description: In all probability "Mike" is the greatest metaphysician since the days of "Doc" Faust. The change of the amoeba, the course of the ion is an open book to him. A discussion concerning heredity is far sweeter to him than the blandishments of a flapper. For this reason "Mike" has become the envy of the undergrads; indeed he must be a superman (influence of Nietzsche) to remain impervious to the wiles of the Dumb Doras. He has a sustained interest in all of those things which make college life worthwhile—only he shuns the female of the species. How do you do it?

name: Bronner, Charles H.
description: Of all the students at Hopkins, the engineers are the most overworked; of all the engineers at Hopkins, the electrical engineers are the most overworked; and of all the electrical engineers, Bronner is the most overworked. In fact, so wrapped up is he in his work that he is seen in public only during his weekly visits to the postoffice. However, if rumors we hear are true, Bronner takes his fling every now and then, and his social successes are as numerous as those of the most popular "deb." This, however, merely illustrates his versatility. Well, good luck to you, "Bronnie." Hopkins will soon be proud of its son.

name: Brown, Charles R.
description: Westminster is a long way from Homewood, but Brownie has never been late for an 8:30. This is not so much due to his good habits, as to the regular service maintained by the Western Maryland. The football season always gives him plenty of room for discussion in his home town because so many of his friends think as he does. We cannot entirely forget his vocation, for, like all engineers, he realizes that his is the only profession. He has not yet chosen his position, but we know that all rich men come from small towns, hence in a few years his advice should be eagerly sought.

name: Burgess, Robert B.
description: Bob is a quiet but pleasant chap, with the natural ability to think and work methodically and effectively. His attainments because of this ability have made him a source of information for his fellow students and have won him the esteem of all his professors. Besides his scholastic endeavors, Bob has also been an active participant in activities and here, too, has made a name for himself. Bob's intention is to become a civil engineer.

name: Calhoun, William P.
clubs: Board of Governors, Tudor and Stuart Club
description: "Bill" blew in from City College in the fall of '21 and immediately proved his worth by making a place in the "Fresh" backfield. His first claim to fame, however, was at the Freshman Banquet, when he proved to be an invaluable asset to one of the entertainers. Since then "Bill" has graced the diamond and the diving board with his Apollo-like form. In recognition of all this he was selected to lead the Class of '25 down the final homestretch to graduation. We feel sure that he will round out successfully a career so well begun.

name: Chambers, Henry Lee
description: The thing that most impresses one when he meets Chambers is his delightful easy-going personality. Both his speech and manner is characterized by smoothness and utter lack of haste. For the last four or five years he has drifted easily through various "fresh-air" courses. Lee is well known as one of the old standbys of the banjo club. His "ker-plunking" during the past few years has helped in no small manner to bring the club up to its present high standard, and he will surely be missed next year.

name: Childs, William M.
description: When it comes to sticking, Melville is the original "Le Page." Stick to it old boy, you will either get it, or it will get you. He is one who firmly expects to be an engineer, that's what he came here for. His accommodating way is a habit well to be followed, this one thing alone stamps him as a gentleman. His friends have great hope for his future, especially when it comes to the ladies, or may I make it singular. We'll all be there anyway, Melville.

name: Cohen, Moses
motto: "You don't know what you're talking about. I just got the dope from Dempster."
description: This is Mose's statement to his fellow students after paying the registrar another daily visit. "Thy voice is celestial melody," but Mr. Dempster no longer thinks so. Mose should worry. He has gathered enough points in three years for his graduation and some to spare for us. Mose intends to study law. We, therefore, wish him success, and hope to hear of him as "the" great lawyer.

name: Cohen, Paul
description: Nobody has ever been able to get any of this company's stock for "love or money." A while back, Paul painted the Philosophy I course green, and it is rumored that Boas' sojourn at Geneva, the following year, was to enable him to recuperate from Paul's strong battering of the exam. Be it for "Kurrley" or "Cartledge," we must admit Paul knows his stuff.

name: Conner, George A.
motto: "Kick" Conner and General Dawes have done more to make pipes famous than Trade and Mark have done for cough drops. "Doc" Adams has "Kick" pose in front of the "Barn" every lunch time, hod in teeth, and declares pipe sales have increased 500 per cent. There is one disadvantage, however, the thoroughfare is blocked by throngs of curious Freshmen who mistake "Kick" for H.R.H. Eddie Windsor.
description: Clothes may not make the man, but a pipe lends dignity to the best of us.

name: Cooke, Raymond W.
motto: "I awoke one morning, and found myself famous."
description: Reverend "Cookie" came to us in 1921, after service in the Army as a second lieutenant. His college education began in 1913 at Taylor University, and has extended over a period of twelve years. While with us he was a crack shot on the rifle team, a debater, and a staunch member of the Christian Service Club. "Cookie" has shown his perseverance by "sticking" to college and overcoming French I. With this perseverance continued we hope that he will be able to say, "I awoke one morning, and found myself famous."

name: Cone, Sydney M., Jr.
description: Sid arrived at Hopkins in his Junior year, and immediately gained fame as a lacrosse player par excellence. He was our goal keeper, and few were the shots that entered the Black and Blue's net when he was around. He modestly states that the game runs in the family. Sid is a likeable chap. He did many of the HULLABALOO writeups just to prove that he was versatile. No, he is not going to be a manufacturer of ice cream as might be expected. Sid plans to enter the cotton business, so some day we will all be wearing Cone's cotton ducks, and cussing Sid for not being a doctor.

name: Conning, Gordon R.
description: The Shelley-like boy from New Jersey. Tall, thin and beautiful. Conning writes fine verse, belongs to the Tudor and Stuart Club, and is bashful. Running the hurdles to keep himself fit, but retiring to his fanciful garden of dreams when the track practice is over, reminds us of Shelley's singular physical energy and retiring mind. And Gordon's sweet face is a mask; he is, in reality, a cynic. If this characterization is inaccurate, it is because the hero is a sphinx.

name: Donaldson, Earl F.
clubs: Black and Blue Jay Advertising Manager, 2, 3
Member of Hopkins-Fifth Regiment Meet Games Committee
Φ Γ Δ Ο Δ Κ Π Δ Ε
description: Donaldson's long list of activities show that he has not been devoid of that spirit of service to class and to University which means so much to Hopkins men. One thing he did will never be forgotten by the undergrads and probably never forgiven by the faculty — he was the class president that appointed the historic committee which staged the notorious and enjoyable "Bowery Ball" in 1924.

name: Dritt, Claude Rice
high school: University Prep. School, Washington, D. C.
description: Everyone who knows Claude can well be proud of his acquaintance, for he is a friend to be valued. He is one of the few to re-enter college after the war, and most of us know of his experiences. Football is his favorite hobby and he can give you the "dope" either past or future about the team. He has big plans after his graduation and we are all hoping to be fortunate enough to be there — we don't mind being surprised but we hate to be disappointed.

name: Ellert, Charles A.
description: In most of our minds there is a small differential space, which, for the sake of clearness, we shall call the feminine space. With a methodical purposefulness, however, Charlie has equipped this area with a complete set of Calculus tables, and a folding slide rule with common sense as an indicator. With such an unusual attribute, it is needless to say that he has emulated the well-known "efficiency curve" in his college work. Conscientious, and a live wire, he will make sparks fly in the electrical work.

name: English, Hugh Y.
description: As an actor, usually of female parts, Hugh is above the average. Literal accuracy compels us to admit, however, that Hugh is not above the average in his other college activities, and has made no deep impression at Johns Hopkins. He is of the stuff that maketh secret societies to flourish. Hugh will be a popular member of whatever society he elects to join, for he is quiet and gentlemanly, and of prepossessing appearance.

name: Fargo, William R.
clubs: A T Ω Ω Β Π
description: We shall always remember Bill for his jovial good fellowship, his limitless number of "good" jokes, his incomparable guitar, and his inordinate pessimism. William is also quite a student, exciting more than once our admiration for his ability to wade through Chemistry, Biology, etc. His versatility is not limited to studies, however; for do not the girls rave about his dancing ability; and was he not one of those brave warriors who saved our class honor on the football field?

name: Frames, Parker W.
description: Ladies and gentlemen, you are gazing at the personification of indolence. Lassitude is his chief characteristic. We can't imagine how Parker comes to be a candidate for graduation; but that is his own secret. Day by day he plods his weary way back and forth over the campus, disturbing little and being little disturbed. But gosh! we almost forgot — he no longer plods, he rides; and in his own racing flivver. However, he's a likeable chap; so we prescribe a little "Pep" for him.

name: Kirsh, Milton B.
description: Big-hearted Milt is generous as they make 'em. He distributes smiles and guffaws in wholesale quantities, and is awfully free with wallops on the back. Haven't you ever been the proud possessor of one of Milt's "loyal Hopkins" trade-marks after a hearty "howdy?" His immediate ambition is to make the med. school. Good work in Chem. and Biology plus good marks all around ought to get him there. But Milt, wise guy, has something in reserve. Why, we wonder, has he always laughed so heartily at "Kürrley's" jokes?

name: Klein, Daniel E.
description: The mere mention of "Danny Loden" Klein connotes to us yellow spats, a checked suit, a brown derby hat, and a big cigar. His proclivities as a politician explain why the political science courses are soft for him. All he does in order to pass these courses is to exercise his talents. Although the HULLABALOO prophecies often go wrong, we are certain that Danny will rise to the dignity of a precinct executive, or even to the high station of a ward politician.

name: Shipley, Oliver M.
clubs: R. O. T. C., First Lieutenant, 4
description: Probably not many of the seniors remember the faithful trio, Arnold, Rawson and Shipley, of a few years ago. During his first year and a half at Hopkins, Shipley was never seen except in company with his two pals; but the mid-year exams will, at times, part the closest friends. Since then Shipley has been alone except for a few new friends, his English Lit. notebook and his deck of cards. There is one thing in which Shipley excels and that is in cards.

name: Shower, Edmund G.
description: Ed is an electrical engineer and well qualified to ring door bells and turn off lights. After noticing the postmark on most of Shower's mail we feel absolutely sure that if there is an opening for a rising young engineer in York, Pa., Ed will at least make a strong bid for the job. So far as is definitely known the sole blot on his escutcheon is the fact that he went to Camp Meade one summer when he didn't have to—but then most everyone knows that Meade isn't far from Annapolis.

name: Gaither, Thomas R.
clubs: R. O. T. C. Officer, Second Lieutenant, 3
description: When Ray was about 10 years old he read that Napoleon had been a little man. About 12 years later Gaither wrought a military success that the R.O.T.C. staff was in a quandary as to what disposition to make of him. Finally they hit upon the happy idea of creating the office of lieutenant colonel for Ray's benefit, and he has acquitted himself well as ranking cadet officer. Gaither never has much to say, but is always doing something—perhaps that's why his career has been so successful.

name: Gardner, Wilson C.
clubs: B Θ Π Ο Δ Κ Cane Club
description: The "C" in the above name would make one think it stood for "charming" after looking at his virtuous countenance. However applicable, as such a sobriquet may be, it must give way to one of more matter-of-factness, for it can really stand for nothing but "chairman." Chairman principally of June Week Committee to be sure, but such a thankless position is but a fitting climax to a career of collegiate chairmanships and such. He is widely known as "Peanuts." Many elections in which he ran for office were lost because he could not print "Peanuts" on the ballot.

name: Birely, M. Franklin
description: Two years ago a slim, fair-haired lad strode into Hopkins, having served his freshman and sophomore years at Gettysburg. Since coming here, Frank has been strenuously engaged in track, tennis, golf, girls, and—incidentally—studies and R. O. T. C. For a while, as far as the fair sex was concerned, he was a typical sailor; now Peabody Institute is his sole port, and many exaggerated rumors are in the air. Frank is a pre-med, ambitious to become a surgeon. With his perpetual smile plus a winning personality, we feel that success is sure, and wish him schooners of luck and happiness.

name: Blome, William J.
description: We have heard of all-American halfbacks, fullbacks, etc., but here is the all-American sleeper. He can sleep through any lecture; even the most interesting fails to awaken him. In spite of this he makes excellent grades in all of his subjects, being an especial friend of Dr. Newlove. The amiable Doctor always claimed that his success in putting Blome to sleep cheered him very much, for here at last was one student who was responding to his teaching. William, however, has shown marked ability outside of the classroom and we feel sure he will succeed in anything he undertakes.

name: Fordyce E. C. Campbell
clubs: Black and Blue Jay Band, Musical Clubs and Student Act. Council, 3, 4; Secretary, 4
description: Here's old "Hump" Campbell who has furnished the harmony on the campus for the last four years. For during that time he has either played in, or lead the undergraduate band, and in spite of what some say, Hump has made it an efficient organization. Fordyce has a musical name, but he has not stuck to music entirely. For some seasons he demonstrated that he had talent in artistic lines, by drawing a goodly proportion of the Blue Jay's cuts. We wonder what will become of the band, now that Hump has gone for good.

name: Crider, Frank B.
clubs: R. O. T. C. Officer, Captain Adjutant, 4
description: When Frank graduates from Hopkins, the University will lose a good man, the engineers will lose a true friend, and Goucher College and State Normal will lose a favorite sheik. Indeed, because of this latter ability, Frank's friends claim that if he fails to make a success as an electrical engineer, he can readily take the place of "Aunt Ada" as editor of the "Advice to the Lovelorn" column. In his spare time Frank has proved himself to be a good student. This is well shown by the fact that he is one of the very few remaining engineers remaining from the original ninety that started out four years ago.

name: Davidson, Jacob I.
description: "Dave" is the engineer; "Jay" is the human being. He took Philosophy I, and after hearing Boas' first few lectures he claims to understand why "academ's" are dizzy. A few moments chatting with "Jay" will show you that he is interested in cultural things. We who meet him outside of class and at the Barn will really miss him. "Dave" will surely reap a reward for his earnestness and long lab. hours.

name: Frazer, Joseph H.
description: Beware of a dark man, says the fortune teller, and we immediately think of swarthy Joe. But we don't get worried, for it is only at a dance that he uses his powers to enthrall the other fellow's femme. In future years we will remember Joe as the man with the iron tennis racquet. He slams the ball so hard that ordinary materials won't stand up under the strain. Joe is the lad who suffers constant breakdowns in Charles street traffic. He is a hearty chap though, so that doesn't embarrass him; consequently we don't expect to hear of any fatal mishap on his part in the years to come.

name: Fringer, John H., Jr.
description: It is remarkable how some men develop in college. A more innocent looking freshman than Fringer never entered Hopkins, but under the tutelage of the O.K.O.'s and his inseparable friend, Don Reese, John has become quite collegiate. In his senior year he came within the narrow margin of one vote of being elected to the student council. The very next Saturday he appeared at the football game dolled up in knickers. No one who knew John in his freshman days ever expected to see him reach the knicker stage. However, college has not changed him in two respects: He has not lost the long, slow stride which 12 years of trodding over the country roads to the Pikesville school gave to him; nor has he lost his genial disposition.

name: Ginther, Joseph R.
clubs: A T Ω Ω B Π
description: His pleasant smile and a smooth, sincere "line" would never lead one to suspect that Ginther is one of the sharpest politicians at Hopkins. But, verily, four short years have transformed "Doc's" formerly soft right palm into a mass of calluses. He can forecast any election three months ahead. "Doc" has quite a reputation as an artist. He was one of the number of bold marauders who so lavishly bedecked the campus during our Freshman year. 'Nuf sed.

name: Gieske, Donald
description: A gentleman of serious and modest demeanor is Don. Quiet and unassuming is his exterior, but underneath is a wealth of that firmer stuff of which men are made. Don has, indeed, made a fine record as a scholar and fellow student. In his studies as in all other undertakings, he has applied himself with keen intellect and arduous determination. To a great many of us, graduation will mean the termination of four years of friendship and association which we will always cherish. With his qualities, Don cannot but attain the uppermost rungs of the ladder of life.

name: Grafflin, Allan L.
description: Allan is one of the few academics who made the grade in three years. He did it with such a healthy average that we are still wondering how it happened. In spite of the strain of scholastic endeavors, Allan is a humorist. He is as lively as a new golf ball. What will the Blue Jay do without his poems on the advantages of necking? Allan will enter the "Med" school. We hope he will never get a chance to attend us, but if we do fall ill, we believe we will be able to rely upon "Doc" Grafflin to pull us through.

name: Griffith III, Robert C.
clubs: Φ Σ Κ Π Δ Ε
First Lieutenant in R. O. T. C.
description: "Bob" introduced himself to Homewood four years ago at the head of a rival contingent of "Johnnies," but after the first visit he swore never to do it again. Consequently he joined the Class of '25 the following year. While at the University "Bob" has endeavored to make the Black and Blue Jay one of the leading humorous magazines, and we must say that he has been successful. His future is assured, since he intends publishing the jokes expurgated from the B. and B. Jay in the form of "Bob's Whiz Bang."

name: William B. Gross
clubs: Y. M. C. A. Services Department
description: Bill has often expressed his opinions over the rostrum, and nearly always the judges have agreed with his powerful voice. In our midst, Bill was also a champion of the Y. M. C. A. Services Department. What boundless good he must have accomplished! We can vividly picture a sinner quaking and begging forgiveness before that torrential rumbling and roaring which Bill is capable of emitting when the occasion demands it. We do not know Bill's plans for the future, but we would suggest that he try lecturing on a Chautauqua circuit, or selling throat lozenges to the natives.

name: Alan Martin Gump
clubs: R. O. T. C. Captain and Adjutant, 3
Black and Blue Jay, Art Staff, 2, 3, 4
description: Of course he's called Andy, but that's only for his name's sake. There is no real simultaneity between this native of Tennessee and his competitor in the comic sheet. Far from it—he is not a blowhard, but a talented youth of much energy who has performed the tasks assigned to him. He is seen every fall in football togs, and, not averse to showing his versatility, may be seen in the spring in lacrosse regalia. If there is a committee for anything, Alan is sure to be on it. As chairman of the Bowery Ball committee, he introduced something at staid old Homewood that will go down in history as the social success of 1924.

name: Harrison, William G.
description: To think that we have to tell you about Bill! Haven't you ever seen him leading yells? Oh, quite right. It is none other than "our boy Bill" whose reputation alone brings countless scented notes to Hopkins, and who is sought by all the ladies from Forest Park to Oshkosh, a full two-cent stamp away. They tell us that Bill will allow nothing to stand as a barrier between him and a good dance, and the greater the distance to the scene of action, the greater the attraction.

name: Hess, George B.
clubs: Ο Δ Κ Π Δ Ε Φ Β Κ
R. O. T. C. Officer, Major, 4
description: This is Hess, the boy who made social psychology famous. George's remarkable mania for asking delightfully embarrassing questions has caused enrollment in this course to double, and cuts drop to zero. He is always happy to answer queries on lovemaking, how to keep a wife, et cetera. It is not this trait, however, that makes George so well liked on the campus. It is because he combines the student, hard worker, and all around good fellow.

name: Pendleton Herring
clubs: Black and Blue Jay Art Staff, 4
description: A quiet chap is Penny, but one who nevertheless gets things done. As a literary light he has made quite a name for himself, and is a member of the Tudor Stuart Club. Likewise Penny has done such good work as an artist for the Black and Blue Jay that in his last year he was made Art Editor. Although we are breaking an editorial rule by doing so, we can't help saying that we are sorry to part company with Penny.

name: Joseph R. Hirschmann
motto: "This is yours, and hence my own; You don't mind if I take it home."
description: There breathes this chap with soul so gay, Who, always, to himself doth say: "This is yours, and hence my own; You don't mind if I take it home." Nor is Joe choicy about the article. He annexes anything from a "light" to your girl at a "prom." Joe is an agreeable student who sits passively in Geology. At unknown intervals, "Volcano Joe" does erupt, and, oh my! didn't he cover Welforth with "exchange rates." "Ozzie" was busy for a week getting untangled.

name: Hofmeister, Jean
clubs: Tennis, Assistant Manager and made letter, 1; Captain and Manager, 2, 3, 4
description: Ah! Beau Brummel and Bill Tilden rolled into one. Hofmeister must be one whopping big man if there is any truth in the old adage that clothes make the man. But anyhow there is no doubt but what he is the best tennis captain that Hopkins has had in three years. If he had taken a little longer to get through, he might have been able to set an unbeatable record. A question, Jean—What college sends tennis challenges in pink envelopes? Hofmeister passes on and the Brooks Brothers-Finchley scholarship is now open to all comers. And likewise the Tennis Team.

name: Holls, Jack O.
clubs: Baseball, Assistant Manager, 2; Black and Blue Jay, 2, 3, 4
description: Radio and heat engines do not mix well according to Jack, and he should know, for he is quite a radio enthusiast. It is hard to understand why a man of his ability with the pen and brush should be on the business staff of the "Jay." But Jack can't help being modest even though the girls try hard to change him. One of his best qualities is his inability to remain angry long enough to become noticeable. His pleasant greeting is one of the things his friends look forward to and which they will always remember. He and Orkney are considering going into business down on the "island" and this we will leave to his own discretion. If the business requires much talking to make it go, you can bet your last cent on Jack.

name: Hoskins, Howell S.
description: He is one of our hardest working and most studious members. He works both day and night. No, no, kind reader don't infer anything from this, for Hos besides his great activity at Hopkins, also gets practical results by teaching night school. However, we hear that there is a nigger in the woodpile, for it is said, "Hos" carries on many activities at night school besides his teaching. But we can excuse "Hos" for this, for we all fall sooner or later. At any rate "Hos" is a good fellow and we predict great success for him in the future.

name: Howard, Philip Williams
description: In the four years he has been with us "Phil" has displayed his ability along many lines. As an embryo economist he has warmed "Tubby's" heart; as a publicist he has shown industry and efficiency; as president of the Student Activities Council he has been a fine executive. But the things for which we like him best are his dry humor and his all around good fellowship. We predict great success in life for him, providing he does not drink any varnish by mistake at a Cane Club celebration.

name: Hunter, James W.
clubs: Y. M. C. A. Cabinet, 1, 2, 3, on Deputation Team, 2, 3, 4
description: Whenever there were tickets or tags of any kind to be sold, Wilse, the demon salesman, was always ready to do his bit. Few indeed were the traveling men, rushing homeward after an 11:30, that eluded his eagle eye. After he accepts his A.B., Hunter intends to apply for Mr. Palmer's job, teaching salesmanship. Hunter takes everything and everybody seriously, even himself. Always bustling, whether after HULLABALOO photos or chasing baseballs during his assistant manager's career. This is a valuable characteristic, certainly, and we may expect to hear more of Wilse in the future.

name: Lloyd W. Hyatt
clubs: R. O. T. C., First Lieutenant, 3.
description: This is "Poco," the diminutive electrical engineer. Like others of his tribe he has for four years worn khaki shirts, drawn beautiful zigzaggy curves of AC current, and rushed madly each week-end to finish preliminary reports. Once a week he would emerge from the "boiler factory" to lay in a stock of supplies. And now that he has thoroughly equipped himself with the fine points of electrical theory he is going to go out and take Tommy Edison's job. If he fails he can always get employment fixing doorbells. Well Poco, whatever you do, we know from our acquaintance with you that you will surely succeed.

name: Robert S. Jacobs
description: Introducing Mr. Rahbut Jacobs, suh of Nawhfulk! Bob left a girl back home. Now, one fall, Bob's very warm missive began bringing back replies which grew cooler with the season. Along about Christmas, Bob got wise and began to describe some snow scenes to her. "And then", said Bob, "those lettuhs began coming back hot enough to melt all the snow around the dorms." Folks, Bob's is a genuine sacrifice—the screen for medicine. Old man, may your service to humanity in medicine be as great as your service in staying off of the screen!

name: Jacobson, Joseph S.
description: Joe is just an engineer—an "electrical" being who spends his days and nights deep in the bowels of the M. E. Building, and comes over to the Barn for a sandwich and a bit of gossip now and then. Of course, Joe is a good student and he knocks 'em all for a goal—including Hulbert. But Joe's seriousness is only skin deep. It covers a bunch of innocent merriment which must have been responsible for many 8.5's appearing as 3.5's, and lots of fellows sitting on thumb tacks.

name: Jaffe, Louis L.
description: Jaffe possesses a happy countenance and a sunny disposition. How can he help it? He spent all his life out on the coast, and for the past three years has pursued knowledge at Stanford, in Southern California. The beautiful environment must have bred in him the desire to know beauty, for Louis is steeped in histories of art and the classics. Our only regret is that '25 wasn't able to enjoy our sunshine until recently.

name: Jennings, Burgess Hill
clubs: R. O. T. C. Officer, Lieutenant, 4
description: Burgess is our musician, the only piccolo player in the band who can secure cash for his "comps." Without his inspiring tenor many a quartet of the mechanical seniors would have fallen flat. Even the profs hate (?) to stop his vocal efforts. He looks so well in a uniform that he wore his twice a week for four years whether he drilled or not (mostly not). As a prelim writer he is a wonder and you can bet on his doing more than his share every time. All of his classmates have great hopes for his future because unlike most engineers he has received credit for many courses on the side. This puts him in a class to himself.

name: Kaufholz, William
description: Here he is, none other than "Moko", the fighting, raring center of the lacrosse team. Bill came out for the team in his Junior year and immediately attracted Father Bill's eye by his cannon-ball goal shooting. Bill's only trouble was that he couldn't keep any teeth in his mouth. First he lost his own and then he lost the dentist's set. But these misfortunes only made him fight the harder to make the team. Here's to you Bill. If you build bridges like you play lacrosse, you'll be successful.

name: Kelly, George L. M., Jr.
clubs: R. O. T. C. Officer, Lieutenant
description: Kelly is a representation of an unusual object d'art—a musical engineer. Add to this base the temperament of the Celt, the proclivities of a sheik and a tendency to wear rather noisy ties—that is a perfect vignette of LaMar. It has been said that after Kelly played every known musical instrument, that he invented a few himself just for the sake of variety. But as he has lived in the same house for at least four years, the conclusion is that he must play well—or else his neighbors support some moving company. And a Chem. engineer is never dirty and grimy.

name: McCallister, James G., Jr.
motto: "There is only one thing wrong with me, and that is I am in love," said McCallister one day in the Chem. lab.
description: We are inclined to believe it, for often we see Mac standing in the laboratory staring vacantly for long periods of time. Even when he joins in the Chem. engineer's daily game of five hundred, he assumes his vacant stare and thoughtful countenance. Apparently he is longing for the night to come so that he can see her again. We do not know if it was anxiety or late hours that caused it, but Mac admits that he lost 10 pounds last winter. We have seen her photograph, so we don't blame you Mac, for your strong affections.

name: McKay, Alfred J., Jr.
clubs: Π Κ Χ Π Δ Ε
Y. M. C. A. Officer, Treasurer, 4
Student Act. Council, Y. M. C. A., 4
Secretary Student Chapter A. S. C. E., 4
description: Mac is that "rara avis," a three-year engineer who is active on the campus. As advertising manager of the "News-Letter" he has saved a lot of writing for the editors with his many advertisements. As the manager of the baseball team he has likewise proved very capable. Al has been with us only three short seasons, but in that time he has made such a place for himself, that he is going to be sorely missed by his former classmates.

name: Thomas P. Magill
description: A book could be written about the All-Maryland Football Player. After knowing Magill one suspects the Irish of being the chosen people. (Tom would be too modest to like that). In four years he has made an enviable record. As a football player he has few equals; the same may be said of him as a good fellow, or even a dangerous one—to antagonize. It has been said that greatness is baffling. So is Mac. Draw your own conclusions. He is probably the most popular man in the class. Undoubtedly he is the most important one. Being captain of the football team and president of the Student Council at the same time is no mean accomplishment. Mac is a rarity.

name: William T. Manahan
clubs: R. O. T. C. Officer, Captain, 4
description: When Billie came to Hopkins from Sabillasville he was a wild country lad. Since coming to Baltimore, however, he has learned only too swiftly and too well the ways of the wild city denizens. Bill has also branched out in other lines. With the rifle he rivals William Tell. In fact, on one occasion he nearly hit another member of the R. O. T. C. in the Adam's apple. However, he reaped his reward in his last year when he was selected to lead the rifle team.

name: Martin, Donald S.
clubs: Σ Τ Δ Ω Β Π
description: He is short and snappy. Don’s countenance has forever been one great burst of sunshine. His cherub smile has cheered us through four years of college. If all his other attributes were worth nothing (which isn’t so) Don’s smile would be worth $1,000,000. Don hopes to become a M.D., but we advise him to become a musician. For several years he walloped the bass drum in the band, and it seems a pity that his talent should go to waste. This is his opportunity to make a big noise in the world.

name: Michel, Edward O.
clubs: Τ Β Π A. S. C. E.
description: Edward is the kind of fellow you read about—scholarly, gentlemanly, and popular among his associates. Although carrying a difficult engineering schedule, he has been an active participant, and enthusiastic supporter, of campus activities and he leaves behind an excellent record. To meet Ed was to like him; and to like him was to come into intimate acquaintance with a fellow of character and ability. Well, old pal, we hate to part with you, but we know you are going forth to do credit to yourself and your Alma Mater in the field of civil engineering.

name: Medinger, Irwin D.
description: It's a shame that we couldn't photograph this lad with a pipe in his mouth. "Medy" and his Dunhill are boon companions. When we think of one we invariably think of the other. All of us do not appreciate Medinger for his true worth. A first impression leaves one with the idea that "Medy" thinks a great deal of Mr. Medinger—yet we can't imagine why. He really is a pretty good scout. And man! how he loves the ladies—and how the ladies love him! No tea dance is complete without him.

name: Milbourne, Charles G.
description: "Cotton" is becoming known among his friends as the late Mr. Milbourne. This is not because he is among the departed, but because he is just usually late in arriving. "Where is Mr. Milbourne?" is a common question asked by the instructors in the 8:30 classes in which he is enrolled. "Cotton" has four ambitions in life. In order of increasing importance to him, they are: To take graduate work in chemistry, to eat plenty, to sleep late, and to play lacrosse. On the lacrosse field Gordon is never late. You can always count on him to be there on time to get the ball.

name: Miller, Karl E.
clubs: Y. M. C. A. Officer, Secretary, 3; President, 4
description: Miller's strange dual nature threatens to make the famous "Dr. Jekyll and Mr. Hyde" case take a back seat. At the Junior Banquet Karl's insidious second nature first asserted itself. One of the entertainers was irresistibly attracted to the hypocritically sedate "Y" president. After this incident he was quietly shadowed and his double life revealed in one of the greatest exposures of the year. (See "Blue Black Mail" of 1924). Moral: Don't let our "Y" cabinet read the "Black and Blue Jay."

name: Mitchell, Alfred K.
description: From the looks of Alfred we would accuse him of being "Byronistic" in flavor. Indeed such a thing is possible, for he is of the student type who takes a deep interest in literature. This does not imply, however, that he is a grind, for if he is, he hides it successfully with a very agreeable personality. Hailing from Virginia several years ago he has with ease, and in an unobtrusive way, succeeded in establishing himself as a fine chap. To say such things usually sound trite, but not in this case. To know the man would dispel such a suspicion. He will probably be one of the members of '25 who will be heard from even more in the future than he is now.

name: Morrill, William Kelso
description: No, ladies and gentlemen, this is not Harold Lloyd, nor is it Raphael without his harp. That angelic look around the eyes combined with that stern and haughty mouth is the result of two years' service as class treasurer, together with many other activities. Singing serenades to the fair ones, and emulating the matinee idols have been two of Kelso's pastimes. When one adds to this the fact that he is both a philosopher and a lacrosse player, the extent of his versatility may be understood.

name: Morrison, Samuel I.
description: Sam is one half of the "Sammie-Akie (Akelytis) bunch." Together they entered Hopkins from Poly and completed their pre-med course here in three years. While with us, Sammie has shown that he is a student. He worked hard to reach his goal, and according to the latest reports, he has succeeded. Sam will enter the Hopkins Medical School next year. Quite an achievement for a Poly boy. With his knack for study, his noble character and quiet way of making friends, Sammie is sure to succeed.

name: Morsberger, Eustis E.
description: The man pictured above is none other than "Useless" Morsberger, but his sobriquet is misleading. "Useless" has two claims to distinction; in the first place, he has completed his college work in three years, and secondly, he is one of the few married men in the Class of '25. Notwithstanding the duties attached to married life and numerous outside activities, Eustace is well known about the campus. We were tempted to say something unkind about his "baby Lincoln," but since he has disposed of it we must practice forbearance. When interviewed last "Useless" promised to send "little Useless" to Johns Hopkins in order to carry on the family tradition.

name: Musher, Sidney
clubs: T A O Φ B K
description: This nice little boy is successfully upholding the family reputation established at Hopkins by his sensational brother. Sidney shows all the earmarks of a Musher—the desire for Spanish 7, the lure of "Tubby," and the irresistibleness of public speaking. Now Sid, we've "spoofed" you enough, old kid. Ours has been a mighty enjoyable acquaintance. So let's see each other again soon and often, 'cause we don't know what we'll do when you're away and we're alone. "What'll we do, what'll we do?"

name: Mylander, Elmer L.
description: Here we have a budding oratorical genius. He expects to go to Harvard Law School next year and after that, to clean up a million or so as a corporation lawyer. Elmer on one memorable occasion made a speech against the United States joining the World Court. He was going strong until he happened to gaze at some of the females in the audience. After that he called the World Court the League of Nations, consequently getting into much difficulty. However, we can forgive him for this and we feel sure he will be successful as a lawyer.

name: Neistadt, Isidore I.
description: The four years that Neistadt has spent with us were spent in hard work. Neistadt hopes to study medicine and "cut 'em up." He is quite clever with the knife. He was once mistaken for an author when he produced "The Cat In Hash." Neistadt is not a grind. (The only grinding, however, that he does, takes place after school in his father's victrola shop). Everyday he is down at the Barn "talking things over." We trust that some day we may hear of Dr. Neistadt's great discoveries. Don't betray our trust.

name: Milton Oliver Ningard
description: Milt could be seen around the campus at any time of the day during the last year, a camera under one arm and a slide rule under the other. He might be termed a photographer by choice, an engineer by mistake. It is calculated that he has used up over 3000 packs of films in snapping various things around the campus. Milt has "shot" everything from "General" Langeluttig to the latest litter of kittens at the "Barn." Among other photos in his collection are Sergeant Frank "without" his broom and whistle, a "standing room only" debate in the C. E. hall, and the Sigma Chi Betas doing something really useful.

name: John P. Otis
description: John expects to be the first mayor of Jessup, Md., and if they ever have need of such an office John is sure to be elected. For if he votes there is nobody to vote against him. Engineering is not the only thing he is interested in, hence we know he will be capable of holding down his job when the time comes. Having such a militant name he is naturally a booster of army training. He even joined the R. O. T. C. the second time, without any consideration; however, he does not refuse his quarterly check. We leave him at this point, but he has invited us around the next time we stop off at Jessup.

name: Parsons, John Warner
motto: "He needs no eulogy, he speaks for himself."
clubs: A. S. B. Board, Junior Delegate, 4, President, 4
description: Gentle reader, look up at "Jawn's" biography and you will learn who he is. Look at his vest, and you can tell that he has been through many battles. "Jawn" Parsons, O.D.K., M.F.P.A.A.B., etc. Gee, what political battles he fought. Promises galore, but fulfillment—Oh hell! we're not in the age of the millennium. Anyway, "Jawn" served his alma mater.

name: Pease, Phillip P.
description: When we look at Phil Pease we are forced to believe in the old adage that "it is quality and not quantity" that really counts. Phil is indeed small of stature, but intellectually he is big, and in respect to personality he is great. If we went as far as to include Y. M. C. A. work we should claim Phil to be magnificent. Pease, we understand, is an embryo doctor of medicine. Confidently we await the day when we can say with pride: "Dr. Pease was a classmate of mine."

name: Pentz, John A.
clubs: R. O. T. C. Officer, Second Lieutenant, 4
description: Angelo has done his best to uphold the honor of his illustrious namesake by contributing sketches to the Black and Blue Jay. It may be said, however, that his ideas of the beautiful did not pass uncensored. (See November copy of Black and Blue Jay). Johnny has never been the same since he took a course in Philosophy 2. At once he began practicing the tenets of Hindu philosophy, endeavoring to reach a state of Nirvana. His success as to the latter is attested to by the way he snores during lectures.

name: Peters, J. William
description: In every Senior Class there are men who have had all the courses in a given subject. Such is the case with "Pete." No, it isn't Poly Con. It is Math! This young Einstein has had them all. In fact he is in Doc Hulburt's office as often as George the janitor. Most of us have some knowledge of figures, but this youth's idea of a good time is to solve differential equations and the like. These pursuits, however, are in keeping with his quiet disposition. He leaves the more material joys of life to the harder spirits.

name: Pledge, John A.
description: His solemn and sedate dignity of conducting the meetings of the Electrical Club in his function as president will never cease to be marvelled at by his classmates who knew him when but a youth. "Al" has never failed, in deed, he not only lends a helping hand, he has more than once "shook the hand" in the well-known "mean" manner. At that, "Al" is a fine fellow—and the gang is pulling for him.

name: Post, Charles G.
high school: Dwight Preparatory School, New York City
clubs: R. O. T. C. Officer, Second Lieutenant
description: Post is well named, he is a veritable giant of a man. Post's characterization of Captain O'Malley in the "Galloper," will long be remembered if only for the reason that his high shakoe made him appear taller than seems possible. Post is no intellectual midget either and it seems probable that he now regrets several earlier phases of existence at the University; notably perhaps, his lack of work for his class in his first years of residence. But he has nobly redeemed himself by his stage presence in the Barnstormers productions and has, it seems, finally come through in fine shape.

name: Reese, Donald
description: Don is good looking and lives near a distillery, which should be enough to account for his popularity with the unfair sex. He was one of the ushers at the De La Mare lectures, which is sufficient reason for Goucher's sudden interest in culture. Seriously, Don is a good representative of Hopkins. He makes no claims to brilliance, but he once got a nine from Gillies. In after years when most of our classmates are forgotten we shall remember Don. He is the kind of man one is proud to call a friend.

name: Regester, Robert T.
description: The most carefree and cheerful of the civil engineers is Bob Regester. He faces the hardest of courses with nonchalance and invariably comes out far ahead of those who toil and grind. Indeed, his ability to quickly grasp involved subjects, has around the admiration of his classmates and even now Bob is well on the way to success in structural engineering. Bob's sparkling wit and ready humor also have done much to brighten the monotony and tediousness of the engineering course and we regret having to part with him now.

name: Reiblich, George K.
description: This young man is a twentieth-century Cincinnatus. During the summer months he follows the plow, but in the autumn and winter he demonstrates that he is the best theoretical soldier in the R.O.T.C. His practical work is seriously impaired because the strongest word in his vocabulary is "Heck!" Notwithstanding, his efficiency as a soldier "Niblick" intends to study law. It is with regret that we see him leave, but it is certain to say that he will prove a credit to his alma mater.

name: Reimer, Charles E.
description: We do not see "Bird" much in the early fall or late spring. If Pimlico and Laurel were not so close to Baltimore, Reimer's attendance might be better. As it is, Ed prefers to spend the balmy spring afternoons at Hill Top rather than at Homewood. We cannot understand how he gets so much work done in the laboratory in the little time he spends there. It must be that he puts into his work that same incomparable neatness that he exhibits in his dress, for there is not a more carefully attired gentleman in the whole Cane Club than Reimer.

name: Rheb, Charles F.
description: Readers, don't let this serene countenance deceive you. Charlie Rheb knows more about the wild night life of Baltimore than any gangster who ever claimed the Folly Cabaret as his hangout. It is rumored that he was led astray while engaged as a cashier at Child's restaurant. Others say he was beguiled into error by Spurrier, the noted cowpuncher. Be that as it may, a noble career is spoiled and the underworld claims another for its own.

name: Rome, Benjamin T.
clubs: Black and Blue Jay Editorial Staff, 2, 3
Member Student Chapter, A. S. C. E., 2, 3, 4
description: Amiable and a gentleman par excellence, Ben has endeared himself in the hearts of his classmates. His unusually high scholarship and finely trained mind are certain augurs of his success upon graduation in the field of civil engineering. Ben's endeavors, however, have not been exclusively along engineering lines. Campus activities, too, have received much of his attention, and his attainments therein are again concrete evidence of his ability. His friends are sure he will bring honor to himself and his school. Good luck to you, Ben.

name: Rudel, Harry W.
description: It is true that "Handsome Harry" was not voted the most popular Senior, but we can assure you that there is not a better liked man among the Senior chemical engineers than Rudel. The Chems. seem to delight in tormenting "Our Harry." However, he takes it all good naturedly. The only time the broad smile fades from his face is when his experiments do not check. Then Harry is one gloomy boy; but when the noon hour comes and he joins in the daily game of five hundred around the lab. tables, his smile comes back again.

name: Sachs, Philip H.
clubs: R. O. T. C. Officer, Lieutenant, 4
Black and Blue Art, Business Staff, 3, Circulation Manager, 4
Winner, Herbert B. Adams Medal for Public Speaking, 3
Φ Ε Π Π Δ Ε
description: Back in 1921 when Phil entered Hopkins, he was very much like any other Freshman—a young, unsophisticated, frightened little boy. Since that time, however, there has been a marked metamorphosis. Indeed, Phil is now truly collegiate, and his knickers, the key on his watch chain—all are indications of the radical change that has occurred. Campus activities, also, have come in for their share of Phil's attention, and both the "Jay" and the Barnstormers have profited by his labors.

name: Sander, George F.
description: The young man pictured above should go down into history as being the greatest expositor of the foreign names of the Goucher dorms. His research work has covered a period of four years, and let it be said, not without attending ills. For several years he has suffered from an acute attack of "Goucheritis" which has centered mainly about the left hip-pocket. George F. has successfully combated with many pretentious rivals while engaged in his research, hence at present he is supreme in his field.

name: Sanderson, T. Harold
clubs: Π Κ Χ Τ Β Π
description: "Sandy" radiates more good nature to the square inch than any man in the C.E. department. He forgets this occasionally, however, in an exciting moment of a lacrosse game, and wields a "nasty" stick as the best of them. Steady playing in the Indian game is not his only forte, for "Sandy" is the final authority as to whether the bridge will behave as it should or topple over. Quiet at times, is this boy, but let no one be deceived. The air actually reverberates at times and becomes slightly hazy. This latter qualification alone should rank him with the best engineers.

name: Sauer, John A.
clubs: R. O. T. C. Officer, Second Lieutenant
description: Fur coats and Stutz cars are synonyms for John. Winter means nothing, but this spring weather is surely not made for him. Several of his fellow engineers feel it quite an honor to help him wear his furs around the campus. If you miss him at noon hour you can find him on Charles street near North. Blue-printing the final reports is his hobby, but try to convince him. When it comes to math, he can be depended on anytime, for in his sophomore year he "sure knew his oats." Hopkins should have another graduate student soon, as it is probable that John will return for more knowledge in the fall.

name: Scherr, Jack J.
description: Jack needs no introduction for his inimitable saxophone playing has made him the idol of the tea fighters of the University. There is hardly a disciple of Terpsichore on the campus who will not vouch that Scherr surpasses Ted Lewis, or any of the other renowned saxophonists. It has been largely through Jack's efforts that the Blue Jay Twin Six has achieved the great popularity it enjoys throughout this section. Jack's major was Political Economy. However, in the words of Doc Mitchell, "Scherr is a damn good saxophonist, but world's worst economist."

name: Scheinman, Jay
clubs: Black and Blue Jay, Editorial Staff
description: The average engineer is supposed to have no liking for cultural subjects, but here we have a civil engineer who, in his spare time, studies art and music. Technical ability, combined with the broadening influence of the liberal arts, indeed augurs well for Jay's success after graduation. Jay has been with us only three years, and we have enjoyed his company all the time. One will have to look far to find as good a fellow.

name: Schlutter, Milton W.
description: Herr Schlutter has inclinations towards poetry and all that is beautiful, and it may also be said that he embodies all of the accomplishments of a Spanish athlete. Countless females have been carried into transports of joy by his poetical works, but none of these prove to be as useful as his latest literary effort, "The Book of a Thousand Excuses." The latter has materialized as the result of his four years in college, and due honor should be paid to this man by future college students for his great work.

name: Shaffer, George S.
clubs: Γ A A B K II
description: Electricity is his pet, but radio is his livelihood. George had mastered all of the fine points of the latter long before anyone thought of putting up an aerial. He cannot see the need for any studies not pertaining to his choice, and just because he works harder than anyone else ever did (?) he firmly believes that electrical engineering is quite a necessity and not to be compared with any other course. The time may come when all radio salesmen will have a certificate proving their worth, and George expects to receive his this June. Well, he is not alone in this expectation, for the rest of us all have the same hope.

name: Shaw, Roger
clubs: Y. M. C. A., Silver Bay Delegation, 3
description: Roger is a man of indisputable courage. Hailing from dear old Princeton at the beginning of his Junior year he has had the intrepidity to introduce the most poignant style of dress ever seen at staid old Hopkins. Red and blue checks mean nothing in his "plus fours." Roger is the original synonym for insipidity. He has captured the interest of everything within a decent radius of Homewood, and everything else he could capture. Since his visit to England last summer he is more English than the English. Aside from that he is a blooming young poet. Note—Blooming isn't used as an opprobrious epithet. Some day soon Roger will come down from up above and visit us awhile.

name: Albert E. Sikorsky
clubs: R. O. T. C., Second Lieutenant, 4
description: "Irish" hasn't made much noise during his stay at Hopkins; we thank the Lord for these rare quiet fellows. Irish has stuck to business; his degree is the result of his concentration. "Irish" made name and fame for himself in an R.O.T.C. sham battle two years ago, when unwittingly he captured his own general, and held him by force. This seems to be his single mistake during four years at Hopkins. Consequently we vote the medal for care to "Irish." He deserves it.

name: Charles C. Singleton
clubs: R. O. T. C., Second Lieutenant
description: "Clayt" is another member of that justly famous group of Senior civil engineers. He has devoted three years of conscientious work to both scholastic and extra-curriculum activities and now deserves the coveted sheep-skin. The successful seasons enjoyed by the Musical Club for the past two years are concrete evidence of his managerial ability and his classmates are here to testify to his being a "good scout." We wish him the best of luck on parting and expect to hear big things of him.

name: Alexander F. Skutch
description: More or less accidentally, Alex elected a course in Botany; in two years he had completed all the undergraduate courses in this subject. This is a remarkable example of a fellow discovering his life work during his college career. Alex is primarily a scholar. His reading has been extensive, he is truly scientific, and, though reserved, he does not look down upon the happy-go-lucky ordinary fellow. Next year he will be an instructor. He will be respected because he knows his stuff, and liked because he is the antithesis of an intellectual snob.

name: Mortimer M. Slatkin
description: Who will ever forget "Murray" as "Grace Whitney?" (Everybody but the actor). Her charm and grace predominated over the entire Barnstormer cast of '24. "Murray" is also quite a comedian. Each noon at the Barn he amuses his friends by blowing "smoke rings" and making "wise cracks." This young man should also be remembered as a philanthropist, for it was he who founded the "Society for the Strengthening of Weak Verbs." He has already enlisted a committee to establish sanitariums where weak and decrepit verbs can be stimulated and vivified. In future years all students of Romance languages will be indebted to him for this humanitarian work.

name: Slowik, Benjamin T. J.
description: Ben contradicts the belief that an athlete cannot play football and still be a scholar. His record on the gridiron is well known, and his scholastic ability is not to be questioned. The same determination that holds him on the line is seen in all his work. Football does not occupy all of Ben's time, and neither do his studies, for he has also been able to acquire much outside experience. Those of us who are less able wonder how he does it. When Ben graduates Hopkins will lose a good lineman, but she will send out just as good an engineer.

name: Smoller, Phineas
description: With a wave of his pudgy hand he causes thrones to totter and kings—and others—to tremble. Pete is most undoubtedly the nearest Hopkins comes to having a James G. Blaine of its own. Pete is a familiar figure on the campus—to all habitues of the Barn and to those that attend class meetings. No election would seem real if it weren't for Smoller and his twelve satellites, the latter usually present by proxy if not in the flesh. Pete says that he likes work. It fascinates him. He can sit and look at it for hours.

name: Spurrier, Oliver W.
clubs: Σ Τ Δ Ω Β Π
description: Ladies and Gentlemen. Step right to the front and get a close up of "Spick" Spurrier, hero of "When a Man's a Man"; the fellow who made cowboy outfits and Herring's "ranch" famous. He may be found at the ranch most any evening, flourishing a revolver, and corralling an enormous number of coca-colas. "Spick" and Miller have compiled a "Who's Who" of every girl between Homewood and Westport, which they keep in the "Y" office for reference.

name: Strasburger, Charles M.
description: "Our boy Cholly" is a favorite byword of the civil engineers. It is safe to say that a great deal of water will flow under the bridge before the C. E.'s forget the carefree good nature of this happy-go-lucky youngster. Engineers undergo quite a few hard knocks before donning the cap and gown and "Skeets" has earned the respect of his fellows by his ability to "come back for more" every time. True or untrue as it may be, the rumor has crossed the drafting tables that "Skeets" is addicted to women of the "not so tame" variety.

name: Summers, T. Stran
description: Ladies and Gentlemen (especially ladies), behold the Sheik of lacrosse players. He even vamps his opponents by hitting them over the head with his stick. Stran is also something of an economist and is a great friend of Dr. Barnett. They have often been seen after class discussing the recent corner on chorus girls or the shortage of stenographers. Stran's two great ambitions are to be a successful business man and to be the twentieth century reincarnation of Beau Brummell. We feel sure he will succeed in both.

name: Tatum, Chauncey R.
clubs: Chairman Committee on the Baccalaureate Sermon
A K Φ Π Δ Ε
description: Irvin S. Cobb never was funnier than our own Chauncey. During the past four years Chauncey has provoked our mirth with his clever witticisms. This was true not only on the campus, but also in classroom. Undoubtedly, Dr. Newlove thinks that Chauncey will become a great accountant, but little does he suspect that the latter is spilling "Tubby's" entire corporation "line" during recitations. If Chauncey enters the business world with the same spirit that he showed while working on the HULLABALOO staff, then we must predict success for him.

name: Thalheimer, Ross J. P.
description: Ross is physically lazy, and we suspected that he was mentally so, too, until he pulled tens in Philosophy, and finished college in three years. We know, now, that his interest in metaphysics is real. The few who have known Ross have found him generous. He has never acquired the college finish; supreme indifference, and lack of sensitiveness, but remains rather shy. Ross goes to Oxford next year, and maybe, will return disillusioned. However, we incline to believe with him, that he really is superior.

name: Tilghman, Richard C.
clubs: Varsity Seal Holder from News-Letter and Musical Club
Secretary and Treasurer of Tudor and Stuart Club
description: "What is to become of the New York 'Times' and the London 'Times' when Mike Tilghman takes over the editorial reins of the Centreville 'Times'?" Simple! There will be three international newspapers instead of two. We do not know whether or not Mike intends to become a journalist; we can only base our forecast on the data in hand.

name: Thomas O. Tongue
description: Thomas Orkney is Solomon Island's first and only mechanical engineer. He still has his sabre and if the motor boat repair business is bad he can organize the militia and live on his military honors. Early at Hopkins he made his mark as a long distance expectorator and he still stands alone in this field. Whistling, strange to say, gripes him greatly, and many a time the lab reports have been on time solely because of his untiring efforts to persuade his fellow classmates to lay off the harmony and stick to A. C. If we tried to enumerate his many good qualities it would take up much more room than is allowed, so good luck Tom, old boy.

name: William Edgar Trout, Jr.
description: From his name one would suspect that the young man pictured above is a poor fish, but not so. "Bill" has an inquiring mind, which never rests until it has found the ultimate cause of all things. It is reported that he has spent many sleepless nights endeavoring to learn what causes the amoeba to have stomachache. Necessarily, Trout is a quiet boy who takes all of his exercise in the "Y" office. The impression that he leaves with us is that of the student who is little distracted by campus activities.

name: Harry Lester Tyler
description: Harry is the boy who delights in mystifying his profs with long and unusual words. In fact, he has acquired quite a reputation in the engineering school for saying nothing at all at very great length. In his spare time, Harry upholds the reputation of the E. E.'s for being the "sheikest bunch" on the campus. Seriously, though, Harry is a very energetic and conscientious worker and should make a name for himself in the field of electrical engineering.

name: Martin J. Urner
clubs: Φ Γ Δ Τ Β Π
description: In physical size Marty is the original "half-pint," but like other great little men of history he carries with him a dynamic, cock-sure appearance that commands our respect. He has a true Epicurean spirit — "wine, women, and song" is his motto; and Marty is a man true to his convictions. His affaires d'amour have been many and furious. Marty's other great weakness is his love for his banjo. And gosh! How he can play. He's almost as good on his banjo as he is in his studies, and that's saying something.

name: Vaughan, Roland G.
clubs: A T Ω Ω Β Π
description: From the desolate, arid wastes of the Arizona desert hails Bucky. Quiet, but deliberate and determined, he has won a great many friends here at the University. Buck is a good "listener," a man to whom we have told our troubles; and one who has given us sympathetic advice. As far as we know Buck has two vices—Pimlico and Chicago. When he is successful at Pimlico he immediately entrains for Chicago; and from what we can gather, boys, oh boys! she must be a beauty.

name: Vogel, Charles E.
description: Where you see Mylander there Vogel is. When Charley is separated from his beloved he goes about with a mournful expression on his countenance saying, "Seen Elmer?" Although he is one of the brighter men of the class, Charley chose lacrosse as his major subject and supplemented this with a few snap minors. It is needless to say that he made good in the first, for he left behind him a wake of cracked skulls. As to the latter, he has shown himself a capable student. Charley considers the reading of the "Harvard Classics" as his greatest achievement while in the University. We heartily agree with this.

name: Waldorf, Sigmund K.
clubs: Black and Blue Jay, Business Staff, 1, 2, 3
description: Here’s where the “academics” are wrong again. They broadcast the charge that the engineering student is a grouchy, discontented, and overworked person; but “our boy Sig” is a pleasant, cheerful, young man, with a winning smile well known about the campus, and despite seeming carefreeness is well up among the leaders in scholastic achievement. Campus activities, too, have come in for their share of “Sig’s” attention, and like his studies, are concrete evidence of his ability. The only bad thing we can say about Waldorf is that he has only spent three years at Hopkins instead of the usual four.

name: Watson, Edward H.
clubs: Φ Γ Δ Undergraduate Scientific Club, 4
description: Edward H. Watson came to the Johns Hopkins University with two motives in view—to become an electrical engineer and to play lacrosse. Ed sent his engineering ambition packing at the end of his second year (it probably interfered with his gaining a firmer and undivided effort to Bill Schmeisser’s lacrosse teachings). It was well worth the sacrifice, for Watson held down a position on the team with great ability.

name: Wienefeld, Robert H.
description: Tis no other than Bob Wienefeld — scholar, humorist, man. "Wiene" realized that he came to Hopkins for an education, and gentle reader, he has gotten it — 9.5's and 10's grow thick and fast in the wake of Bob's scholastic path. He probably rates Phi Bete by this time. Don't get the idea, however, that Wienefeld is a grind; for Bob is popular among his classmates, and grinds are never popular. One reason for his popularity is his everlasting smile. Perhaps that is also the reason of the weakness of the weaker sex whenever Bob is around.

name: Willison, Lawrence D.
description: Worldly-wise, sophisticated man of experience is "Abie." He is the patriarch of the Brooks Bros. family. Beau Brummell is his understudy. Larry is irresistible — to both sexes. At Hopkins he has won the admiration and friendship of both faculty and classmates. At Goucher, and thereabouts, he has — well, guess for yourself. At college nicknames are an indication of affection; and Willison has more than his share. "Abie," "Willie," and "Larry" are some of them. We might call Larry a gentleman, a scholar, or an orator, but better than all these he has been a pal.

name: Willoughby, Carl E.
description: Neatness, precision, and perseverance, these are the qualities that have made for Carl the nines and tens in all of his courses. Carl carries his meticulousness to the "nth" degree. In his desk, every flask, every beaker, every burette has its exact place. "I don't mind lendin' anything just so you put it back where it belongs," admonishes Carl, as his fellow chemists return his bottle of cleaning solution, but put it a half-inch from its "partic'lar" place. Carl is so interested in his work that he does not even stop for lunch. With a sandwich in one hand and a stirring rod in the other he eats and titrates at the same time. A few months of industrial work will cure him of this.

name: Wolf, Edgar F.
clubs: Y. M. C. A., Association Council, 4
description: Friends, this little cherub pictured here is none other than Edgar Wolf, alias "Woof," the pride and joy of the engineers. "Woof's" poker face, together with natural ability, has served him well and has gained a fine reputation for him. We regret, however, that application to his studies has kept "Woof" from participation in other activities, for we feel sure that had we known him more, we should have liked him better. One thing for which "Woof" deserves considerable credit is that he has been unmolested by the women. Well, "Woof," keep up your work and life will be one sweet song.

name: Wong, Harold S. H.
description: Behold the hungry man of Hopkins! Friend Wong came all the way from the Orient to demonstrate his enormous appetite to the citizens of Baltimore. Hungry for food, hungry for knowledge, hungry for the keen enjoyment of life; can no one curb this ravenous young man? Wong came to Johns Hopkins from Princeton, and it is a common belief that he made the change because there was not enough food in New Jersey. The real reason for his changing colleges was to be with Roger Shaw, whose fur coat is such a perfect match for his own. We all hope that the supply of oysters in Baltimore lasts until Wong graduates, for we should hate to see him leave.

name: Young, James R.
clubs: Φ Γ Δ Π Δ Ε
description: Jimmy has not been with us long enough to bare himself to the discerning eye of the biographer. We know, however, that within a short time he showed himself capable of editing the HULLABALOO. Like many of his classmates he has proved to be a master of political finesse, hence we believe he should follow the great game of politics. In some future time the Tammany Hall organization would do well to install him as chief in its wigwam. If recommendations are necessary, just ask any man in the Class of '25.
